Swiss CitiesRheinfelden

Rheinfelden

AGAargau

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The anti-king's island

Agnes, daughter of the anti-king Rudolf von Rheinfelden, discovers a secret passage in Castle Stein on the Inseli as the Rhine rises around her. Torn between loyalty to her father's rebellion and the hope of peace with the Emperor, she must make a choice that will shape the destiny of the Zaehringen dynasty and the future of the city itself.

Create This Story

Rudolf von Rheinfelden (c. 1025-1080), Duke of Swabia, resided at Castle Stein on the Inseli, a rocky island in the Rhine that divides Rheinfelden's bridge into two sections. On 15 March 1077, he was elected the first anti-king in the history of the Holy Roman Empire at Forchheim, challenging Emperor Henry IV during the Investiture Controversy. He was mortally wounded at the Battle on the Elster on 15 October 1080. His daughter Agnes von Rheinfelden married Duke Bertold II of Zaehringen and founded the monastery of St. Peter in the Black Forest, connecting Rheinfelden's history to the dynasty that would later refound the city.

The clock that strikes too early

A child visiting the Obertorturm discovers the clock still strikes seven minutes early. Stepping through the gate at that extra moment, they land in medieval Rheinfelden and must help a farmer reach the gates before the true closing time. Along the way, they learn why seven minutes can make the difference between safety and being locked outside the city walls all night.

Create This Story

The Obertorturm (Upper Gate Tower) of Rheinfelden, first documented in 1256 as the 'Glunkins Gate,' is considered the most important defensive tower of the city's medieval fortifications. Its clock has a famous quirk: it strikes seven minutes early. This was a deliberate design to warn farmers working outside the walls so they had time to return before the gates closed for the night. The practice gave rise to the expression 'Torschlusspanik' (gate-closing panic), still used in German today to mean anxiety about missing a deadline.

Frau Fasnacht's last ride

During Fasnacht, the burning effigy of Frau Fasnacht on the Rhine raft winks at a watching child. She takes them on a wild night ride down the river past the Inseli, the Messerturm, and under the old bridge, showing Rheinfelden's history through carnival eyes. By dawn, the child understands why every winter must end with fire and laughter on the water.

Create This Story

Rheinfelden's carnival tradition centers on Frau Fasnacht (Mrs. Carnival), a figure embodying the spirit of the festival. At the opening ceremony, she is hung from the Obertorturm and 'rules the town' for four days. The festival features parades, Guggenmusik brass bands, Schnitzelbange satirical verse performances, and a children's carnival. At the closing ceremony, participants wearing white scarves accompany Frau Fasnacht from the Obertorplatz to the Schifflande on the Rhine, where she is placed on a raft, set alight, and surrendered to the river. The Fasnachtsgesellschaft Rheinfelden has organized the festival since 1973.

The salt beneath the city

A child follows a trail of salt crystals from the sole uno pool deep underground to the ancient salt deposits beneath Rheinfelden. There they meet the Salzkoenig (Salt King), a spectral guardian of the white gold, who shows them how the 1844 discovery transformed a sleepy medieval town into a thriving spa destination -- and why some treasures are worth more than gold.

Create This Story

In 1844, salt deposits were discovered beneath Rheinfelden, transforming the town. In 1846, the Hotel Schuetzen opened as the first brine bath facility. In 1864, an 'Armensolebad' (poor people's brine bath) was established to democratize access to the healing waters. In 1909, three saltworks merged to found Swiss Saltworks on the Rhine. In 1925, direct brine pipelines connected the salt works to the bathing hotels. Today, the Rheinfelder Natursole is one of Europe's strongest natural brines, pumped from 200 meters depth and piped to the sole uno wellness center, where visitors float in 36-degree water with 12% salt content. Rheinfelden holds official status as a certified Wellness Destination since 2005.

Two Rheinfeldens

A child walks across the old bridge and discovers that stepping past the border stone from 1810 takes them to a time before Napoleon split the city in two. They must reunite two friends -- one Swiss, one German -- before the border falls between them forever, learning that some bonds are stronger than any line drawn on a map.

Create This Story

For nearly 700 years, Rheinfelden was one single city spanning both banks of the Rhine. In 1330, King Ludwig the Bavarian pledged Rheinfelden to the Habsburgs, beginning almost 500 years of Austrian rule as part of Vorderösterreich. In 1802, Napoleon fixed the border on the Rhine: the south bank became Swiss, the north bank became German. A divided border stone from 1810, bearing the crests of Baden and Canton Aargau, marks the international boundary on the bridge. Despite the separation, the two Rheinfeldens remain socially and economically intertwined, with residents crossing the bridge daily for work, shopping, and leisure.

The knights' secret fresco

In the Johanniterkapelle, a child notices a figure in the 1500 Last Judgment fresco is moving. The painted knight leads them through the story of Berchtold von Rheinfelden and the pilgrims' hospital, revealing how noble warriors once disguised themselves as pilgrims to destroy the commandery. Hidden in the chapel wall is a message that has waited five centuries to be read.

Create This Story

Knight Berchtold von Rheinfelden, a servant of the Zaehringen dynasty, founded the Johanniterkommende (Knights Hospitaller commandery) in 1212, originally outside the city walls as a hospital for pilgrims and the poor. Around 1450, the Kommende was attacked and destroyed by noble warriors disguised as pilgrims. Commander Johann Loesel rebuilt it within the town walls, and the chapel was completed between 1456 and 1458 by the city's master builder Herman. The chapel walls feature frescoes from around 1500 in secco technique, including a Last Judgment attributed to a student of Martin Schongauer. Dissolved by the canton in 1806, the chapel has served as an exhibition and cultural space since 1996.

The castle brewery's first brew

Young Theophil Roniger and Mathias Wuethrich argue over recipes while building their 'little castle in the field' in 1876. A child helps them through the crisis of their first harsh winter, when ice had to be hauled by cart and rail all the way from distant Klontal in Canton Glarus. Together they discover that great partnerships are built on passion, patience, and a perfect brew.

Create This Story

Feldschloesschen brewery was founded on 8 February 1876 by Theophil Roniger, a brewer from Magden who trained in German brewing cities, and Mathias Wuethrich, son of a wealthy farmer from Olsberg who provided financial backing. First-year production was 3,600 hectolitres. The brewery's distinctive castle-style buildings gave it its name: Feldschloesschen means 'little castle in the field.' By 1892, it acquired its first mechanical ice maker, ending dependence on natural ice previously transported by cart and rail from Klontal in Canton Glarus. By 1898, it produced 100,000 hectolitres and became Switzerland's largest brewery, a position it still holds.

The salmon of the Messerturm

A child fishing on the Rhine catches an enormous, suspiciously well-fed salmon that tells the dark but comical legend of the Knife Tower's trapdoor. Together they explore the old fortifications, from the triangular Messerturm jutting into the current to the towers along the ring wall, discovering that truth is stranger than legend -- and that even the darkest stories can be told with a wink.

Create This Story

The Messerturm (Knife Tower) stands at the northeastern end of Rheinfelden's old town, directly on the Rhine. It features a distinctive triangular ground plan jutting into the riverbed, its shape designed to 'cut' the floodwaters. The tower served as an interrogation and torture chamber, with an upper room accessible only from the battlements. According to local legend, a trapdoor led to a knife-lined shaft dropping victims into the Rhine. City guides add that the Rhine salmon downstream grew notably well-fed as a result. The tower is part of Rheinfelden's well-preserved medieval ring wall.
